coredata.dc:title: Carbon nitride dots do not impair the growth, development, and telomere length of tadpoles
coredata.dc:creator: Sabol, Anne
Zhou, Yiqun
Zhang, Wei
Ferreira, Braulio C.L.B.
Chen, Jiuyan
Leblanc, Roger M.
Catenazzi, Alessandro
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles, or carbon dots, can have many beneficial uses. However, we must consider whether they may have any potential negative side effects on wildlife or the ecosystem when these particles end up in wastewater. Early development stages of amphibians are particularly sensitive to contaminants, and exposure to carbon dots could disrupt their development and cause morbidity or death. Past studies have investigated short-term exposure to certain types of nanoparticles, but if these particles get into wastewater exposure may not be short term. Therefore, we tested whether chronic exposure to different concentrations of carbon dots affects the growth, metamorphosis, and telomere length of Cuban tree frog (Osteopilus septentrionalis) tadpoles. We exposed 12 groups of five tadpoles each to different concentrations of carbon dots and a control for three months and tracked survival, growth and metamorphosis. We used carbon nitride dots approximately 2 nm in size at concentrations of 0.01 mg/ml and 0.02 mg/ml, known to interrupt development in zebrafish embryos. After three months, we measured telomere length from tissue samples. We found no difference in tadpole survivorship, growth, development rate, or telomere length among any of the groups, suggesting that carbon dots at these concentrations do not disrupt tadpole development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Contaminant
Metamorphosis
Amphibian

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank Diego Salazar for use of equipment. We would also like to thank the Presidential Fellowship at Florida International University for supporting A. Sabol during the course of this experiment. We would also like to thank Maureen Donnelly for her support. The animal study protocol was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee Review Board of Florida International University (protocol IACUC-21-016-CR01).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The total number of surviving tadpoles (jittered) for each treatment type over the course of the experiment.
Body size of tadpoles (snout-vent length, mm, jittered) for each treatment over the course of the experiment. Error bars represent standard deviation.
The number of days for the first tadpole in each group to reach Gosner stage 28 across the three treatments.

Relative telomere length (average T/S ratio) in tadpoles across the three treatments. Points within violin plots show the relative telomere length (average T/S ratio) for each individual that survived to the end of the experiment.
The pairwise comparisons from the Compare groups of growth curves statistical test for average snout-vent length.

head.ce:title: Carbon nitride dots do not impair the growth, development, and telomere length of tadpoles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological Sciences, Florida International University, Miami, FL 33199, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biological Sciences, Florida International University, Miami, FL 33199, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Miami, Coral Gables, FL 33146, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Miami, Coral Gables, FL 33146, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles, or carbon dots, can have many beneficial uses. However, we must consider whether they may have any potential negative side effects on wildlife or the ecosystem when these particles end up in wastewater. Early development stages of amphibians are particularly sensitive to contaminants, and exposure to carbon dots could disrupt their development and cause morbidity or death. Past studies have investigated short-term exposure to certain types of nanoparticles, but if these particles get into wastewater exposure may not be short term. Therefore, we tested whether chronic exposure to different concentrations of carbon dots affects the growth, metamorphosis, and telomere length of Cuban tree frog (Osteopilus septentrionalis) tadpoles. We exposed 12 groups of five tadpoles each to different concentrations of carbon dots and a control for three months and tracked survival, growth and metamorphosis. We used carbon nitride dots approximately 2 nm in size at concentrations of 0.01 mg/ml and 0.02 mg/ml, known to interrupt development in zebrafish embryos. After three months, we measured telomere length from tissue samples. We found no difference in tadpole survivorship, growth, development rate, or telomere length among any of the groups, suggesting that carbon dots at these concentrations do not disrupt tadpole development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made publicly available on OSF at: https://osf.io/du2wp/ upon acceptance of the paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots are a group of carbon-based nanoparticles with a mean particle size between 1 and 10 nm, mostly spherical morphology, abundant and tunable surface functional groups, high surface-area-to-volume ratio, photolumi-nescence (PL), good water solubility, photo, thermal and aqueous stabilities, biocom-patibility, and negligible cytotoxicity (Zhou et al., 2019). Discovery of carbon dots can be traced back to the early 21st century, (Sun et al., 2006) and they can be easily obtained from a variety of precursors via versatile synthetic approaches. They have been extensively studied for their properties and potential applications in various fields such as PL mechanism and structure investigation (Mintz et al., 2021a, 2019), biomedical (drug delivery, bioimaging, sensing, etc.) (Mintz et al., 2018, 2021b; Zhang et al., 2022), chemistry (photocatalysis, thermoelectricity, etc.) (C. Oztan et al., 2021; C.Y. Oztan et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021), engineering (additives, fuels, light-emitting diode, etc.) (He et al., 2020; C. Oztan et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2018), and others (agriculture, etc.) (Milenković et al., 2021).

However, the widespread use of carbon dots raises concerns about their potential to alter environment processes and organisms. Some carbon dots have been observed in nature, (Li et al., 2017; Mandani et al., 2017) and these particles have the potential to end up in the surrounding aquatic ecosystems through wastewater (Mottier et al., 2017) and may affect the organisms living in these ecosystems (Mottier et al., 2017). Our recent study demonstrated that carbon nitride dots, a unique type of carbon dots with clearer structural features, interrupted the embryogenesis of zebrafish leading to the abnormal development of embryos (Zhou et al., 2022a). Although concentrations of nanoparticles in the environment are predicted to be low (Mottier et al., 2017), there is the possibility for bioaccumulation (do Amaral et al., 2019; Mottier et al., 2017), resulting in stronger impacts on organisms at higher trophic levels. Therefore, before we can broadly use any new nanoparticles, we must understand if there may be any hazard for these particles to act as a harmful contaminant on the ecosystem or organisms (Mottier et al., 2017). Many frogs have a unique reproductive mode where the eggs are laid in water, hatch as fully aquatic tadpoles, then develop into semi-aquatic froglets and frogs, therefore waterborne contaminants will affect various life stages differently. Further, a variety of environmental stressors, including contaminants, can interfere with larval development (Altwegg and Reyer, 2003; Lagier et al., 2017; Mouchet et al., 2010), making tadpoles an important life stage to investigate potential new contaminants.

Nanoparticles can result in oxidative stress, genotoxicity, mutagenicity, and embryotoxicity, according to studies across 11 amphibian species (do Amaral et al., 2019). These studies tested a wide variety of nanoparticles of different substances, both organic and inorganic, not only carbon based. Several studies have found some level of toxicity and impaired growth specifically from carbon nanoparticles (Bacchetta et al., 2012; Lagier et al., 2017; Mouchet et al., 2010). However, past studies on carbon nanoparticles generally have only tested exposure for 12 days, whereas many amphibian species take much longer to develop into froglets. If eggs hatch into tadpoles in water containing nanoparticles, there would be chronic exposure for the duration of the development process until they become froglets and can live on land as well. Therefore, a longer exposure time is needed to fully understand how nanoparticles may alter the development of amphibians.

Telomeres refer to the widely conserved repetitive DNA sequences at the ends of chromosomes (Blackburn, 1991) that shorten with cell replication or for a variety of other reasons like stress (Zakian, 1989). These repetitive sequences do not code for any functional proteins and serve as a protective “cap” that are lost to prevent loss of functional DNA (Blackburn, 1991; Zakian, 1989). Telomere length and rate of shortening have been linked to longevity and survival (Bize et al., 2009; Dantzer and Fletcher, 2015; Haussmann et al., 2005; Salomons et al., 2009). However, telomeres in amphibians have been under-studied compared to birds and mammals (Olsson et al., 2018). There are a few initial studies suggesting that telomeres in amphibians shorten with aging (Sánchez-Montes et al., 2020) and certain stressors (Burraco et al., 2017) including ammonium exposure (Zamora-Camacho et al., 2023), and that telomere length is linked to hatching success (Cheron et al., 2021), suggesting a link to fitness. Overall we do not know much about how telomere dynamics work in amphibians and especially in how other contaminants, particularly nanoparticles, may affect telomere dynamics. Thus, this is one of the first studies investigating how contaminants may affect telomere dynamics in amphibians.

Therefore, in this study we aimed to investigate the effects of carbon nitride nanoparticles on amphibian larval development to understand if these particles 1. decreased fitness thorough lower survivorship or negative developmental and growth effects and 2. had any effect on telomere length. We tested these carbon nitride dots on Cuban tree frog tadpoles (Osteopilus septentrionalis) as this species is commonly found in urban, agricultural and natural areas in south Florida, suggesting that they likely come into contact with contaminants. We hypothesize that carbon nitride dots would reduce survival, growth, and development rate as well as result in shorter telomeres of tadpoles.

This study was conducted at Florida International University (Miami, FL, USA) from June to September 2022. We acquired three wild, invasive Cuban Tree frog egg masses from a breeding spot on campus and allowed them to hatch in the laboratory. We found two of the three egg masses on the same day and the third mass four days later. Once tadpoles reached Gosner stage 25 (Gosner, 1960), we separated them into groups of five tadpoles, keeping tadpoles from the same egg masses together in the same groups and aiming for a similar average snout-vent length between groups in the beginning. The experiment with carbon nitride dots consisted of three treatments: a control with no carbon nitride dots, 0.01 and 0.02 mg/ml concentrations of carbon nitride dots developed in Prof. Leblanc's group at the University of Miami (Miami, FL, USA) with approximately 2 nm in size, abundant surface functional groups, good water solubility and stability, and negligible cytotoxicity (Zhou et al., 2022a). We chose these concentrations based on pilot experiments. The lowest concentration matched concentrations at which carbon nanoparticles negatively affected zebrafish and other organisms (Lagier et al., 2017; Mouchet et al., 2010). The highest concentration is double the value of the lowest concentration.

We had four groups of five tadpoles per egg mass and per treatment. Therefore, since we had three egg masses, we had 12 groups per treatment and 36 groups overall. These 36 groups all started with five tadpoles, so we began the experiment with 180 tadpoles. Each group was housed in a 30 oz. glass container (Bovanodo USA) with a plastic lid with holes in the top and a bubbler to oxygenate the water. Each group had 100 ml of water per tadpole (500 ml to begin but the total volume was reduced if any individuals in the group died). We used tap water aged at least 24 h. Once per week we fed the tadpoles rabbit pellets (Manna Pro, Chesterfield), Cobalt Aquatics algae wafers (Cobalt Aquatics, Rock Hill), and added aquatic frog and tadpole food (Zoo Med, San Luis Obispo) in the last month of the experiment. We strained excess food from the water approximately 4 h later and replaced any water that had evaporated with newly aged tap water to keep the carbon nitride dot concentration as constant as possible. The room was kept at an average of 25 °C with 59 % humidity and a 12 h light/dark cycle.

Once per week we measured the snout-vent length of each tadpole with calipers by putting the tadpoles in a plastic bag or Petri dish with a small amount of water. We then also used a magnifying glass to visually identify the Gosner stage of the individual (Gosner, 1960) and then returned it to the group as quickly as possible. After ap-proximately three months (105 days), we euthanized any remaining tadpoles through immersion in a 0.1 % solution of benzocaine until immobile and unresponsive. Tissue was stored in a −25 °C freezer until extraction. All animal work was approved by FIU IACUC, protocol number IACUC-21-016-CR01.

We extracted DNA from the tissue of the entire tadpole using a commercial ex-traction kit (gMAX DNA mini kit for tissue, IBI Scientific, Road Dubuque, Iowa). We quantified relative telomere length (T/S ratio) with qPCR by following the method developed by Cawthon (2002) and the protocol adapted by McLennan et al. (2019) and Cheron et al. (2021). We used RAG1 as the single-copy reference gene (Canestrelli et al., 2021; Cheron et al., 2021) and designed the set of new primers RAG1-F: 5′-GGGCCCTCTGATAGCCGAAA-3′ and RAG1-R: 5′-CATCATATCCTGTACCCCGGA-3′ from RAG1 sequences of O. septentrionalis available in Genbank (accession codes HQ831832–831851). For telomereswe used the primers Telo-1b: CGGTTTGTTTGGGTTTGGGTTTGGGTTTGGGTTTGGGTT and Te-lo-2b: GGCTTGCCTTACCCTTACCCTTACCCTTACCCTTACCCT (Cawthon, 2002). After sample extraction, we used a Qubit 4 fluorometer to measure the concentration of DNA in each extraction and then diluted the samples to 7.5 ng of DNA per 20 ɥl well for the qPCR process (Cheron et al., 2021). Any samples with an initial concentration lower than 7.5 ng per 20 ɥl well were left at their initial concentrations for the qPCR. For standards, we pooled samples from several tadpoles (not treated individuals) not used in the experiment at a known starting concentration with three serial dilutions. We then used the formula for T/S ratio from Burraco et al. (2020), averaging the T/S ratios across all four standards. We ran samples in a QuantStudio 3 qPCR system (Applied Biosystems, Foster City), with samples randomly distributed across three plates, each plate with the same serial dilutions of standards, as well as negative controls for both targets. The qPCR cycles included 2 min at 50 °C and 10 min at 95 °C then 40 cycles of 15 s at 95 °C and 1 min at 60 °C (unmodified, standard SybrGreen protocol). R2 was high (>0.99) for both genes and all plates. Intra-plate variation was 1.38 % for Telo (averaged across all four standards and three plates) and 0.26 % for RAG-1 (averaged across all four standards and three plates). Inter-plate variation was 6.66 % for Telo (averaged across all four standards) and 1.91 % for RAG-1 (averaged across all four standards).

All statistics were conducted in R version 4.2.0 (R Core Team, 2020). To investigate the effects of carbon nitride dots on survivorship for each treatment we ran a Cox proportional hazard model using the package survival, version 3.3-1 (Therneau, 2022; Therneau and Grambsch, 2000). For the growth curves we ran a test called compare groups of growth curves permutation test (Baldwin et al., 2007; Elso et al., 2004) using the function “compareGrowthCurves” in the package statmod, version 1.5.0 (Phipson and Smyth, 2010), specifically designed to compare growth curves of different treatments, on the average snout-vent length with 10,000 permutations. To examine the effects on developmental stage we ran a linear model with the number of days (square root transformed) at which any tadpole of a group reached Gosner stage 28 (the highest stage every group reached) and treatment as the predictor variable. We then ran a linear model with T/S ratio as the response variable (log transformed) and treatment as the predictor variable. We visually inspected the residuals of both linear models for normality.

There was no difference in tadpole survivorship between the control and either of the treatment levels (Cox proportional hazard model, individual predictor results: control vs. 0.01 mg/ml concentration: coefficient = −0.095, z = −0.387, P = 0.70, control vs. 0.02 mg/ml concentration: coefficient = −0.0042, z = −0.017, P = 0.99; Fig. 1 ).
Further, there was no difference in the number of days it took for the first tadpole in each group to reach stage 28 (linear model: R2 = 0.075, P = 0.10; Fig. 3 ) across treatment levels.
There was no difference in relative telomere length between the control and the treatment groups (linear model: R2 = 0.048, P = 0.059; Fig. 4 ).

Overall, we found no effect of the carbon nitride dots on the survival, development, growth, or relative telomere length of Cuban tree frog tadpoles at either concentration we tested (0.01 and 0.02 mg/ml).

We found no effect of either carbon nitride dots treatment on the survival of tadpoles. Because ours is the first study investigating the effects of carbon nitride dots on tadpoles, it is difficult to know whether our findings may apply to other carbon dot concentrations, type of carbon dots, or tadpole species. Previous studies of tadpoles found no effect of other carbon-based nanoparticles on survival (Lagier et al., 2017; Mouchet et al., 2010), and the only studies that did find effects of nanoparticle toxicity was at the embryo stage (Bacchetta et al., 2012; do Amaral et al., 2019), suggesting that the effects of nanoparticles vary by life stage.

We tested Cuban tree frog tadpoles hatched from eggs collected in an urban area, so they may be adapted to handle contamination better than eggs from less urbanized areas, as a study of ammonium exposure found that tadpoles from urban areas and natural habitats responded differently (Zamora-Camacho et al., 2023). Therefore, there may be differences across populations or species in how carbon nanoparticles affect development, but this is outside the scope of this study. Species differences may also explain why carbon nanoparticles reduce growth rate in the model species Xenopus laevis from only a 12 day exposure (Lagier et al., 2017; Mouchet et al., 2010). Further, these past studies only found changes in the growth rate of tadpoles at their two highest concentration levels (0.01 mg/ml, our lower concentration, and 0.05 mg/ml, more than twice our higher concentration, respectively) for only certain kinds of carbon nanoparticles they tested (Lagier et al., 2017; Mouchet et al., 2010). Therefore, these effects in other studies seem to be dependent on both the concentration and the specific particles tested.

Environmental modeling suggests that concentrations of nanoparticles in the environment are expected to be low (do Amaral et al., 2019), though we currently do not know what to expect for true ecologically relevant levels. Further, bioaccumulation could result in higher levels for amphibians and other organisms than the environmental concentration (do Amaral et al., 2019). Overall, these results suggest that testing nanoparticles of different species and at different concentrations is crucial to un-derstand the full ecological impact.

In the wild, tadpoles of Cuban tree frogs can often reach froglet stage in 3–4 weeks, though this time can be longer based on different environmental factors like temperature (Meshaka, 2001). However, in this study, no tadpoles reached the froglet stage after 3 months. Although development can take much longer in the laboratory than in the wild based on both data from preliminary trials and other studies (Lukens and Wilcoxen, 2020), it is possible that conditions in the laboratory were not ideal for development, which may have had a stronger effect on growth and development than the carbon nitride dots. For example, our room was set to 25 °C on average, while temperatures in the wild in Miami during this time were much higher, around an average of 29 °C (Miami-South Florida National Weather Forecast, 2022). We also had relatively high mortality, starting with five tadpoles per group and ending with no more than two tadpoles on average per group at the end of the three-month study. Although some mortality is common during development, it is unclear what expected levels would have been for this species under these conditions and survivorship unrelated to the nanoparticle treatment. Due to the highly cannibalistic nature of this species, we could not examine the tadpoles that did not survive.

Little is known about the effects of stressors on amphibian telomeres (Burraco et al., 2017), with ammonium specifically shortening telomeres (Zamora-Camacho et al., 2023). Additionally, as Cuban tree frogs are highly cannibalistic in the tadpole stage, we were only able to analyze telomere length in tadpoles that survived to the end of the experiment. Therefore, it is possible that carbon nitride dots did not affect telomere length in individuals that survived to the end, while tadpoles that died may have shown different telomere dynamics (though it is important to reiterate that we did not find a significant difference in survival between the control and either level of treatment). Since telomere dynamics in amphibians is a newly emerging area of study (Olsson et al., 2018), more work needs to be done to understand what kinds of stressors, and specifically contaminants, may alter amphibian telomere dynamics.

Overall, we found no effects of carbon nitride dots on the survival, growth, development, or telomere length of Cuban tree frog tadpoles, suggesting that carbon nitride dots did not disrupt the development or cellular processes at the concentrations we tested. However, as with all potential contaminants, we need further testing at other concentrations and on other species to determine the full potential ecological hazard of these particles.

Anne Sabol: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yiqun Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Wei Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Braulio C.L.B. Ferreira: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jiuyan Chen: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Roger M. Leblanc: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Alessandro Catenazzi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: We would like to thank Diego Salazar for use of equipment. We would also like to thank the Presidential Fellowship at Florida International University for supporting A. Sabol during the course of this experiment. We would also like to thank Maureen Donnelly for her support.
The animal study protocol was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee Review Board of Florida International University (protocol IACUC-21-016-CR01).
